triadic_ontology.findings: Mark Pelley's paper 'Triune Agápē as the Ground of Being' argues that the ground of being must be triune, and that agápē is the only fully actual form of relation. Damiano Migliorini's book 'Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics' offers a rigorous and original inquiry into the possibility of formulating a coherent trinitarian theism through the development of a relational ontology and a metaphysics grounded in the doctrine of the Trinity. The Trinitarian Standard Model presents a formal isomorphism between the mathematical structure of the Standard Model's gauge groups and the theological doctrine of the Christian Trinity.

ai_systems.findings: Recent research in AI systems has focused on developing autonomous research pipelines and multi-agent systems. The Mimosa framework, for example, is an evolving multi-agent framework that automatically synthesizes task-specific multi-agent workflows and iteratively refines them through experimental feedback. Another example is the CORAL infrastructure, which is a robust, lightweight infrastructure for multi-agent autonomous self-evolution, built for autoresearch. Additionally, the OMNI-SIMPLEMEM framework is a unified multimodal memory framework for lifelong AI agents, which was discovered using an autonomous research pipeline. These frameworks and pipelines have the potential to automate a broad range of computationally accessible scientific tasks across disciplines.

uap.findings: Recent developments in UAP disclosure and congressional hearings have been gaining momentum, with the Department of Defense releasing UFO files and Congress pushing for greater transparency. The US government has been accused of withholding information about UAPs from Congress, and there have been reports of retaliation against whistleblowers who have come forward with information about UAPs. The Pentagon has changed the names of its UAP investigations, and there have been allegations of a 'secretive arms race' involving UAPs. The US government has been urged to be more transparent about its knowledge of UAPs and to provide more protection for whistleblowers who come forward with information about UAPs.

classroom_ready.title: Lesson 1: Designing an Effective Token Economy System
classroom_ready.summary: This lesson provides a comprehensive checklist for implementing a token economy system in the classroom, including conducting a needs assessment, generating a list of negative and desired behaviors, and creating an action plan.

classroom_ready.title: Character Arena: A Gamified ELA Lesson (RL/RI.10.3)
classroom_ready.summary: This gamified ELA lesson transforms the classroom into an epic battleground where students analyze character motivations, conflicts, and development while earning XP and leveling up their literary skills.

classroom_ready.title: Classroom Token System: 8 ABA Steps to Reinforce Appropriate Behavior
classroom_ready.summary: This post explains how to use a classroom token system based on ABA principles to reinforce appropriate behavior in special education, including individualizing systems and fading support over time.

classroom_ready.title: Token Boards in the High School Classroom
classroom_ready.summary: This guide provides strategies for using token boards to promote self-regulation and motivation in a self-contained high school classroom, including personalizing the reinforcement system and incorporating technology.

classroom_ready.title: A Really Good 90-minute Lesson
classroom_ready.summary: This post breaks down a 90-minute English 10 lesson, including a check-in survey, exit ticket debrief, anticipation work for reading, and a theme review by group, to provide an example of how to approach longer lessons.

pedagogy_pieces.title: The Role of Literature as the Key to Personal Freedom
pedagogy_pieces.summary: This essay explores the idea that literature can be a key to personal freedom, allowing readers to escape the constraints of their own experiences and perspectives.

english_lesson_plans.notes: The search results provided a range of resources for high school English lesson plans, including token economy systems, gamified ELA lessons, and strategies for promoting self-regulation and motivation in the classroom. The results also included an essay on the role of literature in personal freedom, which could be used as a pedagogy piece to inform teaching practices.

intellectual_journals.findings: Sergiu Klainerman, professor of mathematics at Princeton University, argues that mathematical truths precede human discovery and are not created by humans. He believes that mathematics is a discovery, not an invention, and that our job is to uncover these truths. Klainerman's work includes landmark proofs that empty space is stable and that black holes do not disintegrate when perturbed. He also argues that institutions are social technologies that scale trust from personal relations to entire nations, and that good institutions are those that promote cooperation and mutual benefit.

Bertrand Russell's essay 'Useless Knowledge' argues that knowledge and feeling are both necessary for human flourishing, and that the pursuit of knowledge for its own sake is essential for a fulfilling life. Russell believes that the cult of utility has led to a dehumanizing emphasis on practical application, and that we need to rediscover the value of 'useless' knowledge. Gary Snyder, a poet and ecological steward, argues that we need to remake the world by rewriting our stories of the possible, and that this requires a deep understanding of the natural world and our place within it.

The US and Israel's relentless bombing campaign has mostly succeeded in strengthening the Islamic Republic, and the war has returned to the regime a lot of the legitimacy it had lost. The disruption of the Strait of Hormuz has sent markets into convulsions, with the international price benchmark for Brent crude oil briefly surging to nearly $120 a barrel. The Gulf's place in the global oil market was secured in the decades following World War II, and the region's energy companies have become highly diversified industrial giants, anchoring a vast system of production and trade.

Complaints of immigration scams have doubled amid Trump's sweeps, with scammers providing legal advice and fake documentation to desperate immigrants. The scams often rely on the mistranslation of the term 'public notary' to fake credentials. Catholic Charities has become aware of hundreds of such scams, and the organization is working to raise awareness and prevent further fraud.

newyorker.title: Singing the Knicks’ Praises, with a Dash of Metal
newyorker.dek: Doug Berns, a.k.a. DugLust, is a Knicks nut and a modern-day Weird Al, who counts Ben Stiller among his devotees. With the team in the playoffs, he’s busy. Amanda Petrusich writes.
newyorker.text:
Shortly before Game One of the Eastern Conference Finals, in which the New York Knicks hosted the Cleveland Cavaliers, Doug Berns, a thirty-eight-year-old musician who operates under the nom de plume DugLust, was popping in and out of crowded bars near Madison Square Garden, slapping backs and taking pics. Since 2024, Berns has been writing and recording musical recaps of every Knicks game—part postmortems, part devotionals, part roasts—and posting them online.
Berns, who was reared on the Upper West Side, has been a Knicks guy since he was five. “I remember watching the ’94 N.B.A. finals with my brother,” he said. “We were supposed to go to bed, but my brother took this little battery-powered radio into our room and we listened to the end. That memory is foundational.” At M.S.G., Berns, who has a flop of chin-length reddish-brown hair, was wearing an Offline Natives shirt featuring the Knicks point guard Jalen Brunson as a red-eyed demon. Berns went to high school (Dalton) and college (Columbia) in the city and never left. Although he plays bass in a handful of groups, including the Big Woozy Band, which does weddings and other events, he did not anticipate getting into the parody-song business, or becoming a mouthpiece for Knicks obsessives. Shit-talking has always been crucial to sports culture; Berns’s version, in which he rewrites the lyrics to beloved pop songs as howling commentary about free throws, is more silly than caustic. “You can be honest about a poor performance without being hurtful,” he said. “I once told a player to use two hands to catch the ball. But he objectively wasn’t. As fans, we have high expectations, we invest a lot, but these are also human beings who work so hard.” He has become an unofficial team mascot—a folk hero in a bespoke jersey and chain. His “Return of the Mack” recap was featured on “The Roommates Show,” a podcast co-hosted by the Knicks guards Josh Hart and Brunson. (Brunson called it “fire.”)
Berns does not stick to a single style; he has used tracks from ZZ Top, Shania Twain, Smashing Pumpkins, and Outkast as the basis of his recaps. His lyrics usually focus on particularly compelling bits of gameplay, but sometimes they take on a fan’s desperation. “Why? Why can’t the Knicks just beat the Lakers?” he sang in March, over the chorus of Michael Jackson’s “Human Nature.” He said that, as a kid, he was “sickeningly obsessed with Iron Maiden.” His original Knicks plan was to write a heavy-metal song about each game. The melodrama inherent to metal lends itself to the agony and ecstasy of fandom, but he realized that spoofing an existing track was both easier and more resonant.
The Knicks have not won an N.B.A. championship since 1973, and inside the Garden the pre-game atmosphere was vaguely tense. (Sports fandom can be rough on the spirit; “shouting unheard advice in a crowd of unheard advisors,” as Hunter S. Thompson once put it.) Berns got a Diet Pepsi and took his seat. He tries to limit his hollering to preserve his voice for the next morning’s recording session.
The first half of the game had its ups and downs. Then it was mostly downs. With less than eight minutes left in the fourth quarter, the Knicks were losing by twenty-two points. A few already heartbroken spectators began to leave. ESPN analytics had Cleveland’s probability of winning at a grim 99.9 per cent. Berns got agitated when people in the stands started chiding a ref, chanting “Fuck Scott Foster!” He grimaced. “They’re fouling,” he said. “You gotta hold your team accountable.” (Earlier, he’d posted a video honoring Foster; it was reposted by Ben Stiller, a courtside regular, who has also appeared in DugLust videos, playing guitar and drums.)
Suddenly, the Knicks turned it around, beginning the second-largest fourth-quarter comeback in the past thirty years, and sent the game into overtime. The energy in the Garden felt seismic, trembly. When the Knicks won, Berns was dazed. “That’s the greatest basketball game I’ve ever seen,” he said. He took a victory strut down Eighth Avenue, stopping to pose for selfies with fans.
Around six-thirty the next morning, he got to work on his song—a spoof of Chad Kroeger’s “Hero,” a bombastic hard-rocker from 2002—at his home studio in Prospect Lefferts Gardens. He’d been up until three, processing the win. “Jalen Brunson’s the hero who saved us / Hell no, we’re not losing this game,” he bellowed. The urgency of the enterprise keeps Berns from getting precious about the particulars, although he did spend some time perfecting the harmonies on the outro. When the track was finished, at around eleven, he started shooting video. His clips often feature multiple DugLusts, seemingly performing in tandem, an effect he achieves with the Draw Mask feature in Final Cut Pro. He climbed out onto his roof, dragging a tripod and two guitars, and carefully closed the screen so his cats wouldn’t escape. He changed jerseys and sunglasses and angles.
He posted the result around 1 P.M., to cascading likes and comments. The success of the entire project had left him emotional. “You can call me an influencer, you can call me a songwriter, you can call me Weird Al—I don’t give a shit,” he said, his eyes wet. “I just want to be creative. That’s all I’ve ever wanted.” ♦

literary_pick.title: Orbital
literary_pick.author: Samantha Harvey
literary_pick.summary: Orbital is a novel about a secret agent, a thirty-four-year-old American woman of ruthless tactics and clean beauty who is sent to do dirty work in France. The story follows her as she becomes entranced by a mysterious figure named Bruno Lacombe, a mentor to the young activists who believes that the path to emancipation is not revolt but a return to the ancient past.
